Gl 551.notes: Closest star to Sol (1.30 ly). Red dwarf flare star. Hosts Proxima Centauri b, one of the nearest known exoplanets in a habitable zone.

Gl 559A.notes: Primary component of Alpha Centauri, the nearest star system. Wikipedia has one article for the whole system. Sun-like G2V star.

Gl 559B.notes: Secondary component of Alpha Centauri (K1V). The name 'Toliman' is the IAU-approved name for Alpha Centauri B. Wikipedia covers the whole system in one article.

Gl 699.notes: Second closest star system to Sol (1.82 ly). Fastest known proper motion of any star. Red dwarf. Candidate exoplanet Barnard's Star b announced 2018.

Gl 406.notes: One of the nearest stars (2.39 ly). Red dwarf flare star. Famous from Star Trek: The Next Generation.

Gl 411.notes: Red dwarf, one of the brightest red dwarfs in the night sky. At 2.55 ly, one of the nearest stars.

Gl 65A.name: Luyten 726-8 A
Gl 65A.notes: Component A of binary system Luyten 726-8 (Gliese 65). Variable star designation BL Ceti. Wikipedia article is 'Luyten 726-8' (redirected from 'Gliese 65').

Gl 65B.name: UV Ceti
Gl 65B.notes: Component B of binary system Luyten 726-8. Variable star designation UV Ceti. Prototype of UV Ceti-type flare stars. Wikipedia covers the whole system.

Gl 244A.notes: Brightest star in the night sky (apparent magnitude -1.46). A1V main sequence star. Wikipedia covers the whole system.

Gl 244B.name: Sirius B
Gl 244B.notes: White dwarf companion of Sirius. First white dwarf discovered. Wikipedia covers both components under 'Sirius'.

Gl 729.notes: Red dwarf flare star in Sagittarius.

Gl 905.notes: Red dwarf in Andromeda. In about 36,000 years will briefly become the closest star to the Sun.

Gl 144.name: Epsilon Eridani
Gl 144.notes: One of the nearest Sun-like stars. 'Ran' is the IAU proper name, but the star is far more commonly known as Epsilon Eridani. Has a confirmed Jupiter-like planet and a debris disk.

Gl 887.notes: Red dwarf in Piscis Austrinus. One of the closest stars with a high proper motion. Hosts two candidate super-Earth exoplanets.

Gl 447.notes: Red dwarf in Virgo. Hosts Ross 128 b, one of the nearest temperate Earth-sized exoplanets.

Gl 866A.name: EZ Aquarii A
Gl 866A.notes: Component A of the triple star system EZ Aquarii (Gliese 866 / Luyten 789-6). Wikipedia article is 'EZ Aquarii'.

Gl 725A.notes: Component A of binary system Struve 2398 (Gliese 725) in Draco. Both components host exoplanets.

Gl 725B.notes: Component B of binary system Struve 2398 (Gliese 725) in Draco.

Gl 820A.name: 61 Cygni A
Gl 820A.notes: Component A of 61 Cygni, a famous binary star. First star to have its distance measured by parallax (Friedrich Bessel, 1838). Wikipedia article is '61 Cygni'.

Gl 280A.notes: Brightest star in Canis Minor and 8th brightest in the night sky. F5IV-V subgiant/main-sequence star.

Gl 280B.name: Procyon B
Gl 280B.notes: White dwarf companion of Procyon. Wikipedia covers the system under 'Procyon'.

Gl 15A.notes: Component A of Groombridge 34, a binary red dwarf system in Andromeda. Has a confirmed super-Earth exoplanet.

Gl 15B.name: Groombridge 34 B
Gl 15B.notes: Component B of Groombridge 34. Flare star designated GQ Andromedae.

Gl 845.name: Epsilon Indi
Gl 845.notes: Orange dwarf (K5V) in Indus. Has a confirmed Jupiter-mass planet and a pair of brown dwarf companions (Ba and Bb). High proper motion star.

GJ 1111.name: DX Cancri
GJ 1111.notes: Red dwarf flare star in Cancer. One of the faintest known nearby stars.

Gl 71.name: Tau Ceti
Gl 71.notes: Sun-like star (G8.5V) in Cetus. One of the nearest single G-type stars. Has a debris disk and candidate planets. Popular target in science fiction.

Gl 54.1.name: YZ Ceti
Gl 54.1.notes: Red dwarf flare star in Cetus. Hosts three confirmed planets. Possible first detection of a magnetic field from a terrestrial exoplanet (2023).

Gl 273.notes: Red dwarf in Canis Minor. Named after Willem Jacob Luyten. Hosts Luyten b, a potentially habitable super-Earth.

Gl 191.notes: Red subdwarf with the highest known proper motion of any star prior to Barnard's Star. Believed to originate from the Omega Centauri globular cluster.

Gl 825.notes: Red dwarf in Microscopium. One of the brightest red dwarfs visible in the night sky.

Gl 860A.notes: Component A of Kruger 60, a binary red dwarf system in Cepheus. Wikipedia covers the system under 'Kruger 60'.

Gl 860B.name: Kruger 60 B
Gl 860B.notes: Component B of Kruger 60. Flare star designated DO Cephei.

Gl 234A.notes: Component A of the Ross 614 binary system. Low-mass red dwarf in Monoceros.

Gl 35.notes: Closest known solitary white dwarf to the Sun. Third white dwarf discovered (1917). In Pisces.

GJ 1061.notes: Red dwarf in Horologium. Hosts three Earth-mass planets; GJ 1061 d is in the habitable zone. Old star (7+ billion years).

Gl 628.notes: Red dwarf in Ophiuchus. Hosts three confirmed planets; Wolf 1061c was one of the nearest known potentially habitable exoplanets at time of discovery.

Gl 473A.name: Wolf 424 A
Gl 473A.notes: Component A of Wolf 424, a binary red dwarf system in Virgo. Both components are near the hydrogen-burning limit.

Gl 1.notes: Red dwarf in Sculptor. The first entry in the Gliese Catalogue of Nearby Stars.

GJ 3522.notes: Nearby triple star system (two spectroscopic binary components plus an outer companion). Also known as G 41-14.

Gl 83.1.name: TZ Arietis
Gl 83.1.notes: Red dwarf flare star in Aries. Very high flare activity. Three candidate planets reported (2019).

GJ 3618.name: DENIS J1048-3956
GJ 3618.notes: Ultra-cool red dwarf in Antlia. One of the smallest and dimmest known stars near the Sun. Also designated GJ 3618.

GJ 3622.name: LHS 292
GJ 3622.notes: Red dwarf in Sextans. Also designated GJ 3622. One of the faintest nearby stars.

Gl 687.notes: Red dwarf in Draco. Hosts a Neptune-mass planet (Gliese 687 b) discovered in 2014.

Gl 674.notes: Red dwarf in Ara with an exoplanetary companion. Flare star.

Gl 440.notes: White dwarf (DQ type) in Musca. Also known as LAWD 37 / LP 145-141. Fourth closest white dwarf to the Sun. Remnant of a massive B-type star.

Gl 876.notes: Red dwarf in Aquarius with four confirmed exoplanets. First red dwarf confirmed to have a planet (Gliese 876 b, 1998). System exhibits rare Laplace resonance.

GJ 1002.notes: Red dwarf in Cetus. Hosts two Earth-mass planets in the habitable zone, discovered 2022.

GJ 1245A.notes: Component A of triple red dwarf system in Cygnus. Both A and B are flare stars (collectively V1581 Cygni).

GJ 1245B.notes: Component B of the GJ 1245 triple system.

Gl 412A.name: Lalande 21258 A
Gl 412A.notes: Component A of the Gliese 412 binary system in Ursa Major. Also known as Lalande 21258.

Gl 412B.name: WX Ursae Majoris
Gl 412B.notes: Component B of Gliese 412. Flare star designated WX Ursae Majoris.

Gl 380.name: Groombridge 1618
Gl 380.notes: Red dwarf in Ursa Major. One of the nearest K-type stars. Possible unconfirmed planetary companion.

Gl 388.name: AD Leonis
Gl 388.notes: Red dwarf flare star in Leo. One of the most active flare stars known, with emissions detected across the electromagnetic spectrum.

Gl 832.notes: Red dwarf in Grus. Hosts one confirmed Jupiter-mass planet (Gliese 832 b).

Gl 166A.name: 40 Eridani A
Gl 166A.notes: Primary of the 40 Eridani triple system. 'Keid' is the IAU-approved name for component A, but '40 Eridani' is more commonly used. Wikipedia article is '40 Eridani'. The system includes a white dwarf (B) and a red dwarf (C).

Gl 166B.name: 40 Eridani B
Gl 166B.notes: White dwarf component of 40 Eridani. First white dwarf discovered (1910). Often cited as the location of Vulcan in Star Trek.

Gl 166C.name: 40 Eridani C
Gl 166C.notes: Red dwarf component of 40 Eridani. Flare star designated DY Eridani.

GJ 1005A.notes: Component A of a red dwarf binary system in Cetus.

Gl 682.notes: Red dwarf in Scorpius. Two candidate habitable-zone planets were reported in 2014 but not confirmed.

Gl 702A.name: 70 Ophiuchi A
Gl 702A.notes: Primary of the 70 Ophiuchi binary system. Orange dwarf (K0V). One of the first confirmed gravitationally-bound binary stars (William Herschel). Wikipedia article is '70 Ophiuchi'.

Gl 702B.name: 70 Ophiuchi B
Gl 702B.notes: Component B of 70 Ophiuchi (K4V). Highly elliptical 88-year orbit.

Gl 873.name: EV Lacertae
Gl 873.notes: Red dwarf flare star in Lacerta. Young star (~300 Myr) with a magnetic field much stronger than the Sun's. Capable of extremely powerful flares.

Gl 768.notes: 12th brightest star in the night sky. A7V main sequence star. Rapid rotator (flattened shape confirmed by interferometry). Part of the Summer Triangle asterism.

GJ 1116A.name: EI Cancri A
GJ 1116A.notes: Component A of the EI Cancri (GJ 1116 / G 9-38) binary system in Cancer. Near the bottom of the main sequence. Extremely high flare rate (~5 per hour). Wikipedia article title is 'EI Cancri'.

GJ 1116B.name: EI Cancri B
GJ 1116B.notes: Component B of the EI Cancri binary. Ultra-cool dwarf (spectral type M7V).

HIP 67593.name: SCR 1845-6357
HIP 67593.notes: Red dwarf with a T-dwarf brown dwarf companion, in the southern sky. Discovered by the SuperCOSMOS-RECONS survey.

Gl 445.notes: Red dwarf in Camelopardalis. Voyager 1 will pass within 1.6 ly of this star in ~40,000 years. Rapidly approaching the Sun.

GJ 3379.notes: Red dwarf in Orion. Also known as Giclas 99-49. Nearest star in the Orion constellation.

Gl 526.notes: Red dwarf in Bootes. Also known as Wolf 498 / Lalande 25372.

Gl 169.1A.name: Stein 2051 A
Gl 169.1A.notes: Red dwarf component A of Stein 2051, a binary system in Camelopardalis. Wikipedia article is 'Stein 2051'. Nearest known separate red dwarf + white dwarf binary.

Gl 169.1B.name: Stein 2051 B
Gl 169.1B.notes: White dwarf component B of Stein 2051. In 2017, gravitational lensing of background starlight was observed, allowing direct mass measurement.

HIP 58910.notes: Solar-type star (F9.5V) in Coma Berenices. Slightly brighter and more massive than the Sun. At 30 ly it is further than the input data suggests; distance may differ depending on catalog.

HIP 33226.name: Gliese 251
HIP 33226.notes: Red dwarf in Gemini. Also known as Wolf 294 / HD 265866. Nearest star in Gemini. Hosts a confirmed super-Earth (Gliese 251 b) and a second planet in the habitable zone confirmed in 2025.

HIP 83609.notes: No dedicated Wikipedia article found. SIMBAD lists it as SAO 208385. May have uncertain parallax; listed at 5.6 ly in input data but SIMBAD suggests a much larger distance. Possibly a data error in the input catalog.

Gl 205.notes: Red dwarf in Orion. No confirmed planets.

Gl 644B.name: V1054 Ophiuchi B
Gl 644B.notes: Part of the V1054 Ophiuchi / Wolf 630 quintuple star system in Ophiuchus. Wikipedia article is 'V1054 Ophiuchi'. All five components are red dwarfs.

Gl 644C.name: V1054 Ophiuchi C
Gl 644C.notes: Part of the V1054 Ophiuchi quintuple system.

Gl 229.notes: Red dwarf in Lepus. Host of Gliese 229B, the first confirmed brown dwarf orbiting a star (discovered 1995). Gliese 229B was later found to itself be a binary brown dwarf (resolved 2024).

Gl 764.name: Sigma Draconis
Gl 764.notes: 'Alsafi' is the IAU-approved name, but the star is more commonly known as Sigma Draconis. Sun-like K0V star. Wikipedia article title is 'Sigma Draconis'.

Gl 251.notes: Red dwarf in Gemini. Also known as Wolf 294 / HD 265866. Hosts a confirmed super-Earth.

Gl 338A.name: ADS 7251 A
Gl 338A.notes: Component A of the Gliese 338 / ADS 7251 binary system. Two near-identical red dwarfs. Component B hosts a planet. Wikipedia article title is 'ADS 7251'.

Gl 213.name: Ross 47
Gl 213.notes: Red dwarf in Orion. BY Draconis variable (V1352 Orionis). Nearest star in Orion.

Gl 570A.notes: Primary of the Gliese 570 quaternary system (also known as 33 G. Librae). Orange dwarf with two red dwarf companions (B and C) and a T-dwarf brown dwarf (D). The brown dwarf was one of the coolest known at discovery.

Gl 752A.name: Wolf 1055
Gl 752A.notes: Component A of the Gliese 752 binary system in Aquila. Also known as Wolf 1055.

Gl 752B.name: VB 10
Gl 752B.notes: Van Biesbroeck's star. Historically the least luminous and least massive known star (from 1944 until surpassed). Has its own Wikipedia article at 'VB 10'.

Gl 300.notes: No dedicated Wikipedia article found. Faint red dwarf.

Gl 570B.notes: Red dwarf, part of Gliese 570 quaternary system. Forms close binary with component C. Covered in system article.

Gl 588.notes: Red dwarf in Lupus with very stable light flux.

Gl 663A.notes: Wikipedia uses '36 Ophiuchi'. Triple system of K dwarfs. Component A named Guniibuu (Kamilaroi/Euahlayi Aboriginal name for robin red-breast). All three components in one article.

Gl 34B.notes: K7V secondary of Eta Cassiopeiae. Covered in system article with component A.

Gl 34A.notes: Wikipedia uses Eta Cassiopeiae. G0V star similar to Sun. Named Achird (matching data). 472-year binary orbit. Both components in one article.

Gl 285.name: YZ Canis Minoris
Gl 285.notes: Wikipedia uses YZ Canis Minoris. Powerful flare star, roughly three times the size of Jupiter.

Gl 664.notes: Component C of 36 Ophiuchi (also known as 30 Scorpii). Covered in the 36 Ophiuchi system article.

Gl 661A.name: HD 155876
Gl 661A.notes: Wikipedia title is HD 155876 (redirected from Furuhjelm 46). Binary of two similar red dwarfs in Hercules. Nearest true star system in Hercules. Both components in one article.

Gl 908.notes: BY Draconis variable (BR Piscium) in Pisces. Metal-deficient red dwarf.

Gl 663B.notes: Component B of 36 Ophiuchi. Near-identical K dwarf to component A. Covered in system article.

Gl 783A.name: HR 7703
Gl 783A.notes: Wikipedia uses HR 7703. Binary in Sagittarius. Approaching Sun at 129 km/s, closest approach in 41,100 years at 6.7 ly. Both components in one article.

Gl 139.notes: Wikipedia uses '82 G. Eridani' (matching display name). G6V star with 3 confirmed super-Earths. High-velocity Population II star, possibly very old. Dust disk detected.

Gl 783B.notes: Secondary of HR 7703 system. Covered in system article.

Gl 555.name: HN Librae
Gl 555.notes: Wikipedia uses HN Librae as the primary name. Red dwarf in Libra with a super-Earth in habitable zone.

Gl 780.name: Delta Pavonis
Gl 780.notes: Wikipedia uses Delta Pavonis. G8 IV subgiant (evolving off main sequence). Identified as 'Best SETI target' by Turnbull & Tarter. Metal-rich.

GJ 3323.notes: Red dwarf in Eridanus with 2 detected planets (2017). Passed within 7.3 ly of Sun ~104,000 years ago.

GJ 1221.name: G 240-72
GJ 1221.notes: Wikipedia uses G 240-72. White dwarf (DQP9.0) in Draco. 7th closest white dwarf. Massive at 0.81 solar masses. Cooling age 5.69 Gyr.

Gl 896A.name: EQ Pegasi
Gl 896A.notes: Wikipedia uses EQ Pegasi. Binary of two red dwarf flare stars. Primary has one known exoplanet. Subject of 1998 alien signal hoax. Both components in one article.

Gl 644A.notes: Primary component of V1054 Ophiuchi (Wolf 630) quintuple system. Covered in system article.

Gl 784.notes: Red dwarf in Telescopium. Young (~0.85 Gyr). Will approach to 11.4 ly in ~121,700 years.

GJ 1128.notes: Red dwarf in Carina with a prominent 5-year starspot cycle comparable to the Sun's. Distance recognized in 2005.

Gl 581.notes: Famous exoplanet host in Libra. 3 confirmed planets (b, c, e), 2 disputed (d, g). One of oldest, least active M dwarfs known. Subject of intense habitability debate.

Gl 896B.notes: Secondary of EQ Pegasi. M6Ve flare star. Covered in system article.

Gl 268.notes: Binary red dwarf system in Auriga. Variable star designation QY Aurigae. Flare star discovered 1975.

Gl 661B.notes: Secondary of HD 155876 (Gliese 661). Covered in system article.

Gl 338B.notes: Component B of ADS 7251. Hosts an exoplanet discovered 2020. Covered in system article.

Gl 223.2.name: LP 658-2
Gl 223.2.notes: Wikipedia title is LP 658-2 but URL is Gliese_223.2. White dwarf in Orion. 8th closest white dwarf.

Gl 625.notes: Red dwarf in Draco with a super-Earth (GJ 625 b) near the inner edge of the habitable zone, detected 2017 by HARPS-N.

Gl 892.name: HD 219134
Gl 892.notes: Wikipedia uses HD 219134. K3V star in Cassiopeia with 5+ known planets, including 2 transiting rocky super-Earths. Naked-eye star (mag 5.57). Very old (~11 Gyr).

Gl 566B.notes: Secondary of Xi Bootis. Covered in system article.

Gl 829.notes: Double-lined spectroscopic binary of two red dwarfs in Pegasus. Also known as Ross 775. High proper motion.

Gl 566A.name: Xi Bootis
Gl 566A.notes: Wikipedia uses Xi Bootis (Xi Boötis). Nearest visible star in Bootes. BY Draconis variable. Young system (~200 Myr). Both components in one article.

Gl 643.notes: Part of the V1054 Ophiuchi/Wolf 630 quintuple system. Separated ~465 AU from the V1054 Ophiuchi AB pair. Covered in the V1054 Ophiuchi article.

Gl 402.name: EE Leonis
Gl 402.notes: Wikipedia title redirects to EE Leonis. BY Draconis variable in Leo. Also known as Wolf 358.

Gl 667A.notes: Triple star system in Scorpius. K3V primary. All three components covered in one article. Component C has confirmed super-Earths including Cc in habitable zone.

Gl 880.notes: Red dwarf in Pegasus. Earlier planet candidate likely spurious (stellar activity artifact).

Gl 293.notes: White dwarf in Volans. Nearest star in that constellation. 12th nearest white dwarf. Cooling age ~2.65 Gyr.

Gl 667B.notes: Secondary of Gliese 667. Orbits A with 42-year period at ~12.6 AU. Covered in system article.

Gl 667C.notes: M1.5 red dwarf orbiting ~230 AU from AB pair. Hosts 2 confirmed super-Earths (Cb, Cc) with Cc in habitable zone. Covered in system article.

Gl 393.notes: Red dwarf in Sextans with an exoplanet. Chromospherically inactive, possibly ~10 Gyr old.

Gl 105A.notes: Triple system in Cetus. Component A is K-type (HD 16160). Display name '268 G. Cet' is less common than Gliese 105. All three components in one article.

Gl 105B.notes: BY Draconis variable (BX Ceti). ~1200 AU from A. Covered in system article.

GJ 3991.notes: Binary in Hercules: red dwarf + white dwarf in tight 14.71-day orbit. Also known as G 203-47.

Gl 33.name: HD 4628
Gl 33.notes: Wikipedia titles the article HD 4628. K2.5V main-sequence star in Pisces, mag 5.7, just visible to naked eye. Display name '96 G. Psc' is less standard than HD 4628.

Gl 19.name: Beta Hydri
Gl 19.notes: Brightest star in Hydrus at mag 2.8. G2 IV subgiant, a preview of the Sun's future. One of the oldest nearby stars (6-7.5 Gyr).

Gl 68.name: 107 Piscium
Gl 68.notes: K1V star in Pisces, ~6 Gyr old. Mag 5.14-5.26, visible to naked eye.

Gl 53A.name: Mu Cassiopeiae A
Gl 53A.notes: Binary system. High-velocity star moving at 167 km/s through the galaxy. G5V subdwarf primary. One of first high-velocity stars identified. System article covers both A and B.

Gl 53B.name: Mu Cassiopeiae B
Gl 53B.notes: Secondary component of Mu Cassiopeiae binary. Covered in same system article.

Gl 879.name: TW Piscis Austrini
Gl 879.notes: Also known as Fomalhaut B. K-type star gravitationally bound to Fomalhaut despite 0.91 ly separation.

Gl 514.notes: M-type star in Virgo. Has super-Earth Gliese 514 b on eccentric 140-day orbit partially in habitable zone (discovered 2022).

Gl 721.notes: 5th brightest star in the night sky (mag 0.03). Key calibration star. Former and future pole star (~13,700 CE). Has circumstellar debris disk. Rapid rotator at 236 km/s.

Gl 673.notes: K7V orange dwarf in Ophiuchus. In the 'sweet spot' between solar analogs and M dwarfs for habitability studies.

Gl 881.notes: One of the brightest stars in the sky. Famous debris disk. Fomalhaut b was thought to be an exoplanet but is now understood as collision debris. Triple system with TW PsA and LP 876-10.

Gl 915.name: Van Maanen 2
Gl 915.notes: Closest known solitary white dwarf. Third white dwarf ever identified (after 40 Eri B and Sirius B). DZ8 spectral class, prototype for DZ white dwarfs.

Gl 66B.name: p Eridani B
Gl 66B.notes: Component B of p Eridani binary. K2V, 0.76 solar masses. 394-year orbital period. Single article covers both components.

Gl 66A.name: p Eridani A
Gl 66A.notes: Component A of p Eridani binary. K2V, 0.77 solar masses. Two nearly identical stars.

Gl 623.notes: Binary red dwarf in Hercules, 1.9 AU separation. Imaged by Hubble's Faint Object Camera in 1994.

Gl 713A.name: Chi Draconis
Gl 713A.notes: Mag 3.6 binary. F7V primary + ~K0V secondary. System article covers both components.

Gl 178.name: Pi3 Orionis
Gl 178.notes: Wikipedia article title is Pi3 Orionis. IAU-approved name is Tabit (current display name). F6V at mag 3.16 in Orion's club. Spectral anchor point.

Gl 686.notes: Red dwarf M1V in Hercules with one known super-Earth on 15.5-day orbit.

Gl 54.notes: Binary red dwarf system in Tucana with ~1 year orbital period. Nearest neighbors are Zeta Tucanae (3.1 ly) and Beta Hydri (5.1 ly).

GJ 1151.notes: Red dwarf in Ursa Major. Notable for 2020 detection of radio emissions consistent with star-planet magnetic interaction. Confirmed planet GJ 1151 c on 390-day orbit.

Gl 695A.name: Mu Herculis A
Gl 695A.notes: Quadruple star system. G5 IV subgiant primary expanding toward giant phase. Parameters determined via asteroseismology. System article covers all components.

Gl 695B.name: Mu Herculis B
Gl 695B.notes: M4V red dwarf companion in the Mu Herculis quadruple system. Part of Mu2 Herculis pair with component C (~43-year orbit).

Gl 695C.name: Mu Herculis C
Gl 695C.notes: Part of Mu2 Herculis pair (B+C, ~43-year period). Covered in same system article.

Gl 486.notes: Red dwarf in Virgo, formally named Gar (display name matches). Hosts rocky super-Earth Gliese 486 b studied by JWST; dayside ~865 K, likely no atmosphere.

Gl 475.name: Beta Canum Venaticorum
Gl 475.notes: Wikipedia article title is Beta Canum Venaticorum. IAU proper name Chara (current display name). G0V solar analog, spectral anchor point. Name means 'joy' (Greek) or 'beloved' (Latin).

Gl 506.name: 61 Virginis
Gl 506.notes: G7V solar twin in Virgo with three confirmed super-Earth/Neptune exoplanets, all within Venus-distance. Near-solar composition.

Gl 17.name: Zeta Tucanae
Gl 17.notes: F9.5V solar-type star at mag 4.20. Has debris disk detected in infrared. ~5.3 Gyr old.

Gl 877.notes: Red dwarf M3V in Octans; nearest star in that constellation. ~14x more luminous than Proxima Centauri.

Gl 867A.notes: Quadruple system in Aquarius. Gl 867A (FK Aqr) is a spectroscopic binary with strongest known dipolar magnetic field for its mass class (as of 2024).

Gl 867B.notes: Gl 867B (FL Aqr) is a close 1.8-day binary. Companion may be a brown dwarf (~61 Jupiter masses). Covered in same system article.

Gl 222A.name: Chi1 Orionis
Gl 222A.notes: G0V star at the tip of Orion's club. Candidate Ursa Major Moving Group member. RS CVn variable.

Gl 250A.notes: K3.5V star in Monoceros. Binary with M2.5V companion. System article covers both A and B.

Gl 250B.notes: M2.5V companion in the Gliese 250 system. Covered in same article.

Gl 791.2.name: HU Delphini
Gl 791.2.notes: Binary red dwarf in Delphinus with 538.6-day period. Primary is fully convective at 23.7% solar mass.

GJ 2069B.notes: Part of GJ 2069 quintuple system (CU Cancri + CV Cancri) in Cancer. All five stars are red dwarfs. Member of Castor Moving Group. Note: Wikipedia says 54 ly which may conflict with catalog parallax.

Gl 666A.name: 41 G. Arae A
Gl 666A.notes: Primary of hierarchical triple in Ara. G8V, also HD 156274. System article covers all components.

Gl 666B.name: 41 G. Arae B
Gl 666B.notes: Close binary of two red dwarfs (Ba+Bb) in the 41 G. Arae triple. Covered in same article.

Gl 465.name: Ross 695
Gl 465.notes: Wikipedia article titled Ross 695. M2V red dwarf; nearest star in Corvus. No planets detected.

Gl 433.notes: Red dwarf M2V in Hydra with three exoplanets. Gliese 433 d in optimistic habitable zone. Gliese 433 c is nearest/widest/coldest Neptune-like detected (as of 2020).

Gl 785.name: HD 192310
Gl 785.notes: Wikipedia article titled HD 192310 (5 G. Capricorni). K-type star in Capricornus with two Neptune-sized exoplanets.

Gl 216A.name: Gamma Leporis A
Gl 216A.notes: F6V star in Lepus at mag 3.59. Ursa Major Moving Group member. High-priority Terrestrial Planet Finder target. Binary with AK Leporis.

Gl 216B.name: Gamma Leporis B
Gl 216B.notes: AK Leporis, BY Dra variable at mag 6.28. 95 arcsec from primary, visible in binoculars. Covered in same article.

Gl 357.notes: M-type star in Hydra with three exoplanets. Gliese 357 d is a super-Earth in the habitable zone. JWST found no clear atmospheric molecules on planet b.

Gl 150.name: Delta Eridani
Gl 150.notes: Wikipedia article titled Delta Eridani. IAU name Rana (current display name). Mag 3.54, 5th brightest in Eridanus. Name means 'frog' from a historical misattribution.

Gl 451A.notes: Famous high proper motion star ('Flying Star'). Once held the record before Kapteyn's Star and Barnard's Star. G/K subdwarf.

Gl 451B.notes: Companion to Groombridge 1830. Covered in same article.

Gl 849.notes: M3.5V red dwarf in Aquarius with two gas giant companions. First long-period Jupiter-like planet around a red dwarf discovered here (2006).

Gl 502.name: Beta Comae Berenices
Gl 502.notes: G0V solar-type star at mag 4.3 in Coma Berenices. Spectral anchor point. Actually brighter than Alpha Comae Berenices despite Bayer designation.

Gl 137.name: Kappa1 Ceti
Gl 137.notes: Young (300-400 Myr) G5V star. BY Dra variable with 9-day rotation. Excellent match for the young Sun at a key epoch in Earth's past.

Gl 442A.name: HD 102365
Gl 442A.notes: Wikipedia article titled HD 102365 (66 G. Centauri). Ancient G-type star (11-13 Gyr). Previously reported planet likely a false positive (2025 study).

Gl 442B.notes: Companion in the HD 102365 binary. Covered in same article.

Gl 827.name: Gamma Pavonis
Gl 827.notes: F9V solar-type star in Pavo at mag 4.22. Ranked 14th on TPF-C's top 100 targets for rocky habitable-zone planets.

Gl 176.notes: Red dwarf in Taurus (nearest star in constellation). Hosts super-Earth Gliese 176 b orbiting inside the star's inner magnetosphere.

Gl 432A.name: HD 100623
Gl 432A.notes: Wikipedia article titled HR 4458 (HD 100623, 20 Crateris). K0V star in Hydra, ~5 Gyr old. Binary system.

Gl 432B.notes: Companion in HD 100623 system. Covered in same article.

Gl 434.name: 61 Ursae Majoris
Gl 434.notes: G8V solar-type star in Ursa Major at mag 5.35. ~2 Gyr old, 93% solar mass.

Gl 569A.notes: Flare star in Bootes orbited by a pair of brown dwarfs (Gliese 569 Ba/Bb in 2.4-year orbit). Complex hierarchical system.

GJ 3192.name: LTT 1445 A
GJ 3192.notes: Primary of the LTT 1445 triple system. Hosts at least 2 confirmed rocky exoplanets (Ab and Ac), making it one of the closest transiting exoplanet systems known (22.4 ly).

GJ 3193B.name: LTT 1445 BC
GJ 3193B.notes: Unresolved binary pair (B+C) in the LTT 1445 triple system. B and C orbit each other every ~36 years, while the BC pair orbits A at ~34 AU separation.
